real_questions: What impact, if any, are recent AI rollouts and high interest rates having on your ability to fundraise?

real_questions.text: Redemption requests are elevated across the industry — what are you seeing in your funds, and in non-traded structures specifically?
How would valuation declines and weak flows move through your earnings?
What are your redemption terms against the 5%-per-quarter benchmark, and how much of your capital is locked up or permanent?
What are your AUM retention rates, split by fee-generating versus custody-only assets?

real_questions: How are you appealing to the younger generation, and what is your client retention plan?

common_pitfalls.text: Redemption terms looser than the ~5%-per-quarter benchmark — treated negatively from a credit perspective.

strong_answer_markers.text: Frames permanent and long-dated capital (drawdown funds, permanent vehicles, insurance-linked) in exactly one way: little redemption risk.

agency_intel.text: Alt manager earnings stayed strong through Q1 2026 despite choppy markets; private credit concerns are mostly isolated to software/tech exposures (BDCs especially) with little impact on overall results. The strain point agencies watch: elevated redemption requests, driven by AI disruption risk in software companies and broader private credit concerns. Expect probes on earnings growth, realizations, and redemption requests in non-traded BDCs specifically.
Fitch's current focus for traditional investment managers: valuation declines, weak flows, and fee pressure.

The fund's ability to lock up capital is critical — so capital doesn't leave the day performance turns. Benchmark: ~5% per quarter withdrawable; looser is a negative. LP concentration is secondary — employee ownership plus LP capital blunts it; don't over-prepare it relative to redemption terms and fee durability.
Agencies give more rating power to fee-generating assets than custody-only assets — present retention by fee-generating cohort.
